name: Spinlock

x.coordination: Mutual exclusion by busy-waiting: a thread repeatedly tests an atomic flag (with CAS / test-and-set) until it acquires the lock, never sleeping.
hazards: Wasted cycles while spinning (no useful work)
Disastrous if the holder is preempted (priority inversion / convoy)
Cache-line contention / coherence traffic under load
Must not be held across blocking or long sections

def: A lock that acquires by busy-waiting on an atomic flag rather than sleeping, trading CPU cycles for ultra-low latency on very short critical sections.
problem: For tiny critical sections, the cost of parking/waking a thread (a sleeping mutex) exceeds the wait itself.
mechanism: Acquirers loop on an atomic test-and-set / CAS until the flag is free. Good implementations spin on a local cached read (test-and-test-and-set), add backoff, and may fall back to sleeping under contention.
benefits: Minimal latency for short, uncontended sections
No syscall / context-switch on the fast path
Useful in interrupt / kernel contexts where sleeping is forbidden
limits: Pathological if the holder is descheduled
failures: Holding a spinlock across I/O or a long computation
Priority inversion when a low-priority holder is preempted
Convoy / lock-step thrashing on contended cache lines
whenNot: Long or blocking critical sections (use a mutex)
User space with unpredictable scheduling and high contention
myths: "Spinlocks are faster than mutexes" — only for very short, low-contention sections
